source.title: Counterfactual Credit Assignment in Model-Free Reinforcement Learning
source.venue: Proceedings of the 38th International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML)

confidence_rationale: Mesnard et al. (2021) address the credit assignment problem in RL by separating what was imagined (the agent's hindsight-conditional estimate of what would have happened under a counterfactual action) from what was realized (the actual return). The core architecture trains a future-conditional value baseline that estimates the counterfactual return -- functionally equivalent to a pre-commit simulation channel -- while the policy gradient update uses the difference between actual return and this counterfactual baseline as the error signal, which is the realized-error channel. An independence loss enforces that the counterfactual baseline does not mix information that is only available post-action, which is the computational analogue of write-boundary enforcement. This architecture supports MECH-060 because it provides an existence proof that separating imagined-outcome estimation from realized-outcome error is both architecturally tractable and empirically beneficial -- the separation reduces policy gradient variance and improves learning. The paper does not operate in a harm-attribution domain, and the write-boundary here is an optimisation constraint rather than a safety constraint, but the computational structure is directly analogous.
